data.title: 2 Blue Chips I'm Buying After April's Insane Market Volatility
data.description:
During a two-week family wedding vacation in Cancun, I worried that this crazy trade conflict might explode, setting the world aflame upon my return.

"I hope my readers and subscribers can handle the craziness that's surely going to happen in the two weeks I'm gone," I thought before I left.

And indeed, there was a lot of craziness, just not the kind I expected.

In the weekly economic update (coming this weekend), I'll review the specifics of the trade war and real-time economic data.

But today I wanted to share my wonder at the shocking turn the markets took and offer you five incredible blue-chip bargains you don't want to miss.

What Just Happened?

Consider the month of April, a truly historic month for volatility.

  • Three days after Liberation Day, the S&P fell 12% in three days!
  • Goldman Sachs reported an eight-standard-deviation extreme selling event on Thursday... followed by even bigger selling pressure on Friday! (1 in 350 billion event).
  • VIX hit 60 on April 7, something that has only happened 34 times since the creation of the VIX (In 1990).
  • "Pause" rally of 10% in one day was tied for the eighth best single-day market rally in history.

The next day? At one point, the S&P was down 6.26%, giving up 64% of the previous day's rally.

Four of the 32 most volatile days in stock market history occurred within the same week following Liberation Day and the "pause."

And within days, the market began a nine-day winning streak, the longest in 21 years. And the strongest nine-day winning streak in stock market history.

Soon after that streak broke, a six-day winning streak began, which was broken on Monday, April 21, with a modest decline.

And now we've just seen a historical 22% rally off the lows set on April 7 (intraday -4.6% S&P low, -21.3% below Feb. 19's record close).

And what about volatility? Remember me pounding the table on April 7 and that entire week about how a VIX of 60 is an excellent time to buy stocks?

VIX 60+ = Be quiet and buy something smart and you'll thank me in a year!" Dividend Sensei April 7

The Most Important Bear Market Chart You'll Ever See

%
VIX Threshold Number of Daysof Trading Days Notable Periods Forward 1-Year S&P Return Forward 5-Year S&P Return 5-Year CAGR
VIX 50+ 75 <1% 2008-2009, 2020, 2025 +35% +129% 18.02%
VIX 55+ ~45 ~0.5% 2008-2009, 2020, 2025 +38% +135% 18.64%
VIX 60+ ~30 ~0.33% 2008-2009, 2020, 2025 +40% +140% 19.14%
VIX 65+ ~25 ~0.28% 2008-2009, 2020 +42% +145% 19.63%
VIX 70+ ~18 ~0.2% 2008-2009, 2020 +45% +150% 20.11%
VIX 75+ ~12 ~0.13% 2008-2009, 2020 +47% +155% 20.59%
VIX 80+ ~5 ~0.06% 2008-2009, 2020 +50% +160% 21.06%

(Source: CBOE Data, Macrooption)

When the VIX hits 60 (always for terrifying reasons), the average 12-month gain (100% success rate) is 40%, with the average five-year return 140%, a Buffett-like 19.1% CAGR.

Over the last six weeks, we've seen the fastest decline in stock market volatility in history.

OK, these are some impressive and bullish charts, but what if this is a bear market rally?

History Is Clear: This Is Likely A New Bull Market

The reason I love reviewing financial charts for several hours each day is that the most intelligent people in finance put things in context for all of us Wall Street nerds.

Warren Pies of 3Forteen Research crunched the numbers and discovered that if this is a bear market rally (that fails to end in a new record high but eventually hits new lows), it would be the fourth largest in history.

If this is a bear market rally, it would be unprecedented (regarding retracement). Doesn't mean it can't, but it would be unprecedented if it did.

The most significant bear market rally retracement since WWII is 60% in October 2022 (right near the end of that -28% bear market).

Again, unprecedented things can (and do) happen.

Negative Oil Was Unprecedented Until April 20, 2020, When It Happened

But the point is that, barring an actual recession, which is no longer the base case, April 7 was most likely the bottom of this bear market and the start of a new bull market.

So what am I buying after a crazy two weeks for the market?

What does "buy something smart and thank me in a year" look like?

Let me show you.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE): The Best Biolife REIT Is A Buy

This is the kind of chart that makes me think of two things.

Either A, the best office REIT in America (actually a biolab REIT) is somehow broken, or this is a classic Buffett-style "fat pitch."

So what's step one? Checking what happened in 2021, when the 65% bear market began.

  • A four-year bear market!

ARE traded at the peak of the speculative "meme stock" era at 29X FFO, compared to a 10-year average of 19.5X.

  • 50% historical premium!
  • Same S&P overvaluation as March 2000 (tech bubble top, worst market bubble in US history).

In other words, 50% historical premiums are the official DK "potential trim/sell" point because historically, when such valuation premiums are reached, long bear markets, where the price can fall like it has for ARE, become highly likely.

OK, but ARE is approaching a 70% decline, and historically, 44% of stocks that fall 70% or more, never recover, ever.

Why Alexandria REIT Isn't A Likely Value Trap

FFO has been growing at 14% CAGR for the last four years. OK, but what about FFO/share? Maybe ARE was diluting investors like crazy?

OK, so what does this mean?

Not a Value Trap

Period FFO/share CAGR AFFO/share CAGR Dividends/share CAGR
2021–2024 6.85% 9.74% 5.05%
2024–2028 1.25% 2.31% 3.18%
2021–2028 3.62% 5.42% 4.11%

(Source: FactSet Research)

Are dividends expected to be cut in the future?

Nope, there are no dividend cuts from a BBB+ rated blue-chip with 14 years without a dividend cut (expected to reach 17 years by 2027).

What about extreme leverage? Rating agencies want to see 6X or less for a REIT like this. In the short term, leverage is expected to peak at 7.2X annually, 6.2X in 2025, and 6.1X in 2026, followed by 5.4X in 2027 and 3.7X in 2028.

OK, so what's the deal? Why does a stock start 50% overvalued but then fall 66%? Four years of declining share prices despite solid growth and safe dividends?

Why Wall Street Hates ARE But You Shouldn't

Healthcare is in a bear market for numerous regulatory reasons right now.

Metric Q1-25 FY-25 Guidance (mid-pt) 5-yr avg Comment
Occupancy (North America) 91.7% ~ 90–91% 96% Oversupply/expirations
AFFO per-share 2.30 (Q1) 8.61 9.25 Mid-point trimmed 0.07 $this quarter
Dividend ($/yr) 5.28 5.32 e — 14-yr CAGR 6%
Payout ratio (AFFO) 57% 61% 67% Wide cushion
Net-debt / EBITDA 5.9× ≤ 5.2× target by 4Q 5.4× Leverage creep but within the IG range
Liquidity $5.3 B — — Only 13% of the debt matures through 2027

(Source: ARE Q1 Results)

Wall Street is concerned that NIH budget cuts might mean lower occupancy for biolabs. Indeed, management has reduced guidance to 90.5% occupancy compared to a five-year average of 96%.

But in this industry, 90% of AFFO payout ratios are safe. ARE's payout ratio is 57% this quarter, and management is guiding for 61%. The five-year average was a very low-risk 67%.

Today's dividend is better covered than ever since 2020, at much higher prices and lower yields.

Consensus Return Potential: Upside Potential This Year and Within 5 years

Potential is not a forecast! I'm not predicting 150% gains by year-end on ARE. If ARE grows as management expects this year and returns to long-term historical market-determined fair value, if it's up 150% by year-end, that's 100% justified by fundamentals.

Oh yeah! Remember how I pounded the table on Realty Income at a 6% yield in January?

  • Why Realty Income's 6% Yield Makes It A Must-Buy For Dividend Investors

How did I know that Realty Income was close to its final bottom of a 6.2% yield just weeks after I wrote this article?

I didn't have a crystal ball. What I did have was our Zen Research terminal confirming the thesis was intact, that Realty wasn't a value trap.

A 6% yield was the 88th percentile over the last decade, meaning you could achieve that just 12% of the time.

And 6.2% (the peak this cycle) was the 93rd percentile.

Do I know that ARE is near the bottom? Nope.

But is it undervalued?

  • 63% undervalued!

ARE is so undervalued that if it were to double overnight, it would still be undervalued.

Yes, it's growing slowly (2.6% long-term consensus), but do you know the 20-year growth rate? 2.9% CAGR.

In other words, ARE historically trades at a 3.2% yield and 19.5X FFO as long as it grows at 3%.

Given its No. 1 position in biolabs, I'm willing to put my money where my mouth is and add Alexandria Real Estate to my family's ZEUS portfolio as a small starter position. I'll set limits to buy more to chase it to the final bottom.

My ARE Limits (One Already Filled)

I've already taken a starter 100-share position on ARE at an insanely attractive 7.51X FFO multiple.

  • 19.52 10-year average.

ARE is 63% historically undervalued, If it falls a bit more, I'll buy 1,500 shares (worth close to $100K).

  • 0.2% starter position and rising to around 3% if these limits are filled.

For context, 65% undervalued is the most undervalued I've ever seen in Ultra SWAN quality companies (ARE is a Super SWAN). But blue-chip quality is good enough if the valuation is attractive enough and the stock isn't a value trap.

For example, according to the Ben Graham rule of thumb, ARE trades as if priced for -2% CAGR growth forever.

The growth consensus is 2.6%, so that's a very attractive 11% long-term return potential with significant short to medium-term upside potential.

But what if I told you there was a blue-chip that was an even better "Buffett-style fat pitch" bargain?

AES Corp. (AES): 4.7X Earnings And Not A Dumpster Fire! Priced For -8% Growth And Management Is Guiding For 8%

AES has been falling for about two years, and is down 62% from those highs.

AES might not seem to have started out overvalued, but it was, trading at 17.5X earnings back in 2022 when the 10-year average PE is 12.2.

  • Do not question the market over 10-plus year periods.
  • If AES averages 12.2X PE over time, it's worth 12.2X earnings.

4.7X Forward Earnings! = Pricing in -7.6% CAGR Long-Term Growth

AES is a BBB- investment grade credit rating Super SWAN utility.

The consensus of all 17 analysts covering AES (a utility) is for 5.2% long-term growth.

  • Management's 2027 growth guidance is 7% to 9%.
  • Mirror image of what the PE is pricing in.

Is AES A Value Trap? NO!

Period EPS/share CAGR FCF/share CAGR Dividends/share CAGR
2022 – 2024 13.2% 10.0% 4.66%
2024 – 2028 2.59% 5.64% 1.07%
2022 – 2028 6.00% 7.11% 2.25%

(Source: FactSet)

Slow growth is fine when you're paying less than 5X earnings! When a stock is priced for almost -8% growth, forever growing 1% to 2% (5.2% long-term) is good enough to make for some highly attractive returns.

The company uses a lot of non-recourse (mortgage-like debt) just like Brookfield (BAM). But S&P adjusts for that and estimates a debt/EBITDA of 3.6X vs 4.5 for utilities it likes to see.

The company is guiding for 6% long-term growth, even better than what analysts expect.

OK, so no dividend cuts are expected, the payout ratio will be 38% in the next year (70% is a safe payout ratio for utilities), and it's trading at sub-5X earnings.

  • Ben Graham level multiples!
  • In the modern era!

What does that look like?

We have a 6.3% low-risk yield while you wait for consensus total return potential of 140% by the end of 2025 and possibly 230% within five years.

AES is NOT the world's most significant utility - the average utility trades at 20X earnings long-term, and AES trades at 12.2X.

But today it trades at 4.7X!

OK, I get it. It's cheap, not on fire, and the dividend is so well covered that the dividend payout ratio would remain safe even if earnings were cut in half (76% payout ratio).

But why does Wall Street hate AES with such a passion?

AES: Not A Buy And Hold Forever Company By Any Means

Metric AES Value Implication
Forward P/E 4.7× Reflects deep risk discount
Dividend Yield 7.0% More than double sector average
Payout Ratio 38% of adjusted EPS Well below 50% safety threshold
Dividend Growth Track 13-year streak; 2–3% guidance Consistent but moderating growth
Free Cash Flow –$4.6 B (2024) Negative; funds via asset sales/debt
Debt-to-Capital 77% High leverage vs. 52% peers

(Source: FactSet)

AES is not a regulated utility but a hybrid utility that owns merchant power assets, selling power for whatever the market will pay.

That's why its payout ratio is much lower than most utilities. Due to more volatile earnings, it has to maintain a stronger safety buffer.

AES's historical beta (volatility vs. S&P) is 1.42, 42% higher than the market's and about 2X higher than that of the typical regulated utility.

Currently, AES is working on a $12 billion growth backlog, which is why management expects to grow EPS by 5% to 7% CAGR long term.

But it's more reliant on falling interest rates (which keep getting pushed back by the Fed over tariff fears).

S&P isn't worried, but if its green energy-focused growth backlog (which relies on tax credits) doesn't pan out, the currently very low-risk dividend could become high-risk and even get cut.

Remember what I said about dividend cuts?

AES cut its dividend once before and brought it back in 2013.

Is it any surprise that AES fell 97% at one point as in a 25-year bear market that's not going to end anytime soon (likely not for decades)?

How long will AES break even from its 97% crash in 2000 if management delivers on its 6% growth guidance (and 13% CAGR total returns)?

  • 12.53 years.
  • October 2037
  • 37-year bear market!

What about inflation?

That 78% decline from October 2000s record highs is actually an 88.36% decline from the record high.

This means that management's 13% CAGR total return guidance from here (7% yield plus 6% EPS growth) would take 15.7 years, if management delivers on its guidance.

That would mean AES would go from a record high in October 2000 to a new total return price in January 2041.

  • A 41-year bear market when adjusted for inflation.

What the heck happened in 2000?

AES 2000s Disaster: A Case Study In Finite Game Thinking

Simon Sinek's Book The Infinite Game discusses how life is an infinite game that never ends. Like most managers, finite thinkers focus on the short term, such as quarterly profit targets or traders trying to earn a quarterly or annual bonus.

But for those with long-term thinking, a "righteous cause" that motivates us to get up in the morning, more than just earning a paycheck, we understand that investing, rather than trading, is an infinite game.

Infinite Game companies are those built to endure for decades or centuries.

What Does an Infinite Game Business Look Like?

Company Founded Age (years)
Bank of New York (now BNY Mellon) 1784 241
Genda Shigyō 771 1,254
Hōshi Ryokan 718 1,307
Sennen no Yu Koman 717 1,308
Nishiyama Onsen Keiunkan 705 1,320
Kongō Gumi 578 1,447

(Sources: company websites)

AES was not founded or run like an Infinite Game company. It was initially run like a meme stock.

In 1981, AES was founded as a developer of industrial power plants.

It went public in 1991 and began an empire-building plan that spanned 32 countries, including Argentina, Brazil, Venezuela, Hungary, Pakistan, and others.

It built merchant power plants, and Wall Street loved AES. It grew to a peak market cap of $28 billion, and many investors thought its business model was the future of utilities.

  • "Utilities can be growth stocks when AES runs them!"

To give you an idea of how "non-utility-like" AES was, from 1998 to 2001, it grew its power generating capacity from 14.5 GW to 50 GW, a 60% CAGR.

In the age of the tech bubble, 60% growth (fueled by $22 billion in debt) was red-hot.

High rates? 6% 10-year yield? Who cares! The debt is floating and will come down at some point!

CEO Dennis Bakke believed in a highly decentralized, high-flying corporate culture. Small groups of managers would have wide latitude to grow at any cost, and grow AES did.

What gets measured gets managed. And growth was what stock option-fueled pay was compensated for, so managers ensured AES grew. Because it took on greater and greater risk. What started out as a traditional regulated utility focus became a focus on merchant power and global emerging markets.

These strategic decisions - high leverage, merchant market exposure, and emerging market bets - were profitable in the late 1990s but left AES extremely vulnerable when conditions turned.

And turn they did. 65% to 85% of projects were funded with recourse debt (the parent company was liable for the debt).

  • Today, 70% of debt is non-recourse.
  • Why a company with 10X debt/EBITDA has 3.7X actual corporate leverage and a BBB- investment grade credit rating.

A Fragile AES was battered by a global collapse in merchant power prices because a decade of debt-fueled capacity led to a glut.

Next, in some markets (like the UK) new regulations caused power prices to fall 40%.

Meanwhile, 30% of sales were from emerging markets, including Venezuela, Argentina, and Brazil.

Argentina defaulted on its debt, devaluing the Peso, which crushed cash flow. Brazil didn't fare much better due to historic droughts impacting its hydropower plants. This emerging market chaos turned one of the hottest growth markets into a dumpster fire of losses.

The collapse of Enron in 2001 caused capital markets for merchant power producers (battered by years of falling power prices and tanking stock prices) to slam shut.

Throw in the California power deregulation crisis and 9/11, and investors fled AES by the boatload. The price fell from $70 at its peak to just $2.

What About AES Today? Dirty Value But Not A Value Trap

Since the IPO in 1991, adjusted for inflation, AES was up 20X at its peak in nine years.

You can see why it was considered a Wall Street Darling. And then after a 97% decline (plus a 50% decline in the Russian default crisis, a 73% decline in the GFC, and another 32% in the Pandemic).

Adjusted for inflation, $1 invested in AES in 1991 is $2.28 today, and $1 invested in the S&P is $12.87.

  • 2.45% CAGR real return since inception (34 years!) for AES (less than bonds!)
  • 7.8% CAGR S&P returns.

Ok, but hasn't management learned from the horrific decade of bad decisions of the 1990s (including mountains of recourse debt).

Today, AES is less risky than its growth-at-all-costs years, but it still maintains a much higher risk profile than most utility investors are comfortable with.

Segment Description Type Revenue Contribution Traditional US-like Stable Utility
Contract Generation Electricity production under long-term power sales agreements is designed to minimize exposure to commodity prices. Merchant power 71.3% (merchant total) No
Competitive Supply Wholesale electricity sales in competitive markets with a lower proportion of long-term contracts. Merchant power 71.3% (merchant total) No
Large Utilities Regulated utility operations provide stable, rate-based cash flows, primarily in the United States. Regulated utility 28.7% (regulated total) Yes
Growth Distribution Electric distribution businesses in emerging markets are operating under long-term concessions. Regulated utility 28.7% (regulated total) No

(Source: CSIMarket, Alpha Spread)

71% of revenue is from non-regulated utility segments, which explains why the historical PE ratio is 12, while a regulated utility like Duke Energy (DUK) is 18X. An aristocrat utility (ConEd) is 18.4X.

A 33% lower multiple is the market correctly pricing in AES's higher risk profile.

For most of its history, AES was a junk bond company, and only in 2022 did it achieve investment-grade credit ratings from all three rating agencies.

More Conservative Approach To AES Buys for ZEUS

Unlike Alexandria, where I'm willing to be more aggressive, AES's higher risk profile (dirty value) means I require the price to fall around 10% before buying the same amount.

Do I think AES is going to fall this much? No, it fell 10% on the day I wrote this (Wednesday, May 21), so I'm setting limits higher than the closing price. After hours, AES is up about 1.5%, and I don't want to risk missing out on a starter position.

  • Make sure to size your positions and limits appropriately.
  • If AES limits fill, it will be about 3% of ZEUS.
  • My personal risk cap on AES is around 7.5%, 50% of the 15% risk cap we recommend for Super SWAN quality companies.
  • If all 15 limits above are filled, that would be a 7.5% position in AES.
  • Because of the company's "dirty value" history.

Bottom Line: No Matter What Happens With The Stock Market Next, Don't Forget About The Infinite Game

Don't forget that the stock market is the ultimate "Infinite game." Capitalism never ends, and whether you have a good or bad year doesn't matter; enduring long enough to achieve your goals and protect your family matters.

Warren Buffett's skill is investing, but his secret is time. - Morgan Housel

You aren't just investing for your own financial goals, but also those of your heirs, whether they be your children or charities you care about.

As we saw on April 7, the "long-term" can be reduced to just 12 months after a VIX 60 event.

That is why I'm so excited to keep all my readers and subscribers motivated and optimistic.

Yes, the economic damage due to tariffs hasn't yet shown up.

Yes, the earnings impact hasn't shown up yet.

But for those willing to look beyond just the next few weeks or months, history is clear.

Barring a recession or an unprecedented bear market rally, April 7 was the low.

And what about between May 2025 and April 2026? That's where buying smart things comes into play!

You can buy so many smart blue-chip stocks, both individual companies and ETFs!

It's always and forever a market of stocks into a stock market, and for those who view global capitalism as a way for providing for their "infinite game" dreams?

Well, the world has never looked brighter. Because as long as the world doesn't end and capitalism endures, investing in a diversified blue-chip portfolio is the ultimate "Infinite game" move for you and your loved ones.

I can't tell you whether ARE or AES will deliver on the upside return potentials that their current fundamentals justify.

But I can tell you this: If a year from now, the S&P is up 15% from here (the 40% average gain from VIX 60), then I would expect both ARE and AES to be up a lot more than 15%.

For those wondering what "something smart" looks like for me? ARE and AES are perfect examples of Buffett-style "fat pitch" deep value high-yield blue-chip opportunities.

I don't have to pray for luck because with disciplined financial science like this, I make my own luck.

data.title: Worried About Social Security? Consider These 3 Stocks To Fund Your Retirement
data.description:
Introduction

Will Social Security be around a decade from now? No one knows and is something people have worried about for years. Ever since I was a young child, I've heard about Social Security potentially going away.

While retirees may be on edge about whether this will happen or not, dividend investing is a way to help relieve some of your stress if this is your current situation.

It's stressful working for most of your life just to hear that this may not exist when it's your time to retire. I recently had a conversation with my uncle who is nearing retirement and this is something he worries about.

In this article, I list 3 stocks perfect for retirees, whether you're currently collecting Social Security or looking to in the not-so-distant future.

Will Social Security Go Away?

According to a survey by Merrill Lynch, 60% of 18- to 44-year-olds think Social Security will not be around by the time they reach retirement age.

No one knows what the future holds. But one thing I'm sure of is prices will be a lot higher than they are now due to inflation. And Social Security may not be enough in retirement.

Most of us likely need less money in retirement. But there are some bills that have to be paid no matter what. And with inflation, those bills increase over time. I mean, how much less expensive was your light bill 20 or 30 years ago?

For some, this includes medical expenses, some type of insurance, and maybe even mortgages.

For my uncle, he stated he was retiring this upcoming November as he will have reached the full retirement age and expects to collect a total of $3,500 a month or $42,000 annually.

For some, this may be sufficient depending on where you live. In some states like California, Massachusetts, Hawaii, or New York, $42k is not enough to cover the annual cost of living average of $51,000.

For my uncle, he lives in the state of Alabama, which also happens to be one of the top 10 states with the lowest cost of living. So, for him, $3,500 will go pretty far.

But after our recent conversation, he told me he's probably going to keep working until next year. Honestly, I've heard this story for years, and he's probably going to keep going until the day he dies.

I tell him he should retire and enjoy the life he has left. But for some, work is all they know.

But if you're a dividend investor like me, or looking to become one, then you can boost your income in retirement from companies that pay dividends. So you don't have to worry about working for most of your life to cover your expenses.

As most know, dividends aren't guaranteed. However, investors should take a quality over quantity approach instead of chasing high yields. While these can be great over the short term, some could also be more risky as their high yields could be sending a warning signal.

However, some quality companies are known for their higher yields. Take Altria (MO), Verizon (VZ), and REITs like Realty Income (O) & VICI Properties (VICI) for example. On the contrary, BDCs (BIZD) often have higher yields, sometimes into the double-digits but bring a higher level of risk.

Why Retirees Should Consider BDCs

I'm often blown away when many investors tell me they are not familiar with BDCs. I've also met a lot who were not familiar with REITs (XLRE) as well, but from my experience, the REIT sector is more familiar to investors.

Like REITs, BDCs were also created by Congress but 20 years later, in 1980. The difference is that BDCs act as banks or lenders for companies that don't have access to large financial institutions. They borrow debt at fixed rates and lend at floating rates.

And this is why they're highly sensitive to the direction of interest rates. When interest rates were hiked their fastest in history from 2022 to 2023, many BDCs enjoyed nice share price appreciation and rewarded their shareholders with dividend increases as well as paid-out specials and/or supplementals. At least the higher-quality ones.

On the flip side, lower interest rates can also cause underperformance as BDCs will see lower portfolio yields, meaning lower income. But the higher quality ones are likely to still perform and pay solid dividends despite compressed earnings.

Some BDCs are still solid long-term holdings due to their strong fundamentals, making them deserving of a spot inside an income-focused portfolio.

I wouldn't weigh too much of your portfolio towards the sector due to it being riskier than most Blue-Chip stocks, but I would sprinkle in a few depending on your retirement needs.

Without further ado, here are the 3 stocks I would add to your portfolio for a reliable income stream.

#1 Ares Capital (ARCC)

The first BDC I would add to an income-focused portfolio is ARCC. Currently, the largest BDC by market cap, ARCC boasts size & scale advantages over some of its peers with its $27.1 billion portfolio. This grew by $4 billion from the year prior.

I also like that they are the most conservative BDC in the sector, focusing on growth instead of conducting frequent dividend increases and paying out supplementals.

Their current yield of nearly 9%, fiscal conservatism, and growing portfolio are why I think they're the top BDC for income. ARCC is also known for its large amount of spillover income, which typically rolls over into the upcoming year.

Coming into 2025, Ares Capital had $922 million, or $1.37 in spillover income. In their latest quarter, this was $883 million.

They also have one of the best balance sheets in the sector, with a debt-to-equity ratio of just 0.98x, well below the peer average of 1.19x. Additionally, ARCC received an upgrade to its credit ratings from Moody's & S&P this past September & November.

ARCC usually has some of the highest NAV growth in the sector, which for BDCs, typically translates to higher share price growth.

This is also a reason they usually trade at a premium vs. some peers. In their latest quarter, their NAV grew to $19.82, up from $19.53 the year prior.

ARCC has not raised its dividend since the end of 2022 but has a solid track record of 15 years of consecutive dividend payments.

Moreover, with their amount of spillover income, shareholders could potentially see an end-of-year special dividend this year.

Dividend coverage has decreased from the previous year, standing at approximately 113%. This was in comparison to 123% the prior year's quarter, an obvious result of lower base rates impacting overall portfolio yields.

But as previously mentioned, ARCC's experienced management, ample liquidity, and interest rates potentially remaining higher for longer make them a great portfolio addition.

#2 Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL)

Blackstone Secured Lending is still fairly a new BDC, IPO'ing in 2021.

As stated in their name, the BDC is managed by one of the largest asset managers in the world, Blackstone (BX). So, it has some size, scale, and liquidity advantages as well.

Despite their short track record, BXSL has become a major player in the sector with strong fundamentals and a 9.7% dividend yield.

Like Ares Capital, they're also externally managed, meaning a higher likelihood of them being a bit more conservative. They've kept their dividend steady at $0.77 since 2023, likely a result of this and increased economic uncertainty.

But still, the BDC has continued to have impressive showings. In their latest quarter, their portfolio grew an impressive 20% from the previous year. This stood at $12.8 billion, up from $10.4 billion.

As a result, NAV growth followed, growing solidly from $26.87 to $27.39. Like Ares Capital, they also saw a lower portfolio yield than the previous year, which caused net investment income to decline from the previous year.

NII amounted to $0.83, giving BXSL solid coverage of 108%. This stood at $0.87 in the prior year's quarter. Like ARCC they are also investment-grade rated and usually trade at a premium above their net asset value.

This is important because it gives them an advantage over some peers, as they could access additional capital by issuing shares. Issuing shares at a premium above NAV usually results in growth.

BXSL's leverage ratio was in-line with the sector median's 1.19x, increasing from 1.03x the year prior. But as previously mentioned, this shouldn't alarm investors as Blackstone Secured Lending also focuses more on growth, similar to Ares Capital.

Unlike ARCC, however, their portfolio has a more defensive posture with a much higher focus on first-lien loans at 98.2% compared to 58%.

But ARCC's larger size and liquidity help balance an income portfolio that includes both ARCC & BXSL.

They also had spillover income in their latest quarter. Management didn't specify the amount but would like to focus more on growth instead of returning it in the form of dividends.

#3 Capital Southwest (CSWC)

Last on the list is the internally managed CSWC, which also happens to be the most shareholder-friendly BDC on the list. This, along with their frequent dividend increases and supplementals, makes them a great portfolio addition.

They're also the smallest BDC on the list, with a portfolio worth $1.8 billion. Like their other two peers, CSWC also saw some decent portfolio growth year-over-year, up from $1.5 billion the previous year. Despite NAV experiencing a slight decline from $16.77 to $16.70, it grew from the previous quarter's $16.59.

They are also investment-grade rated and had a conservative leverage ratio, the lowest on the list, at just 0.89x. Unlike some BDCs, Capital Southwest seems to focus more on equity investments, in which they're able to harvest gains from their equity portfolio.

On a pre-tax basis, NII year-over-year declined from $0.68 to $0.61. But dividend coverage remained solid at 105%. Including their supplemental of $0.06, their bottom line failed to cover the $0.64 payment in dividends.

However, management remained confident in their ability to reward shareholders, again confirming the $0.06 supplemental. This is because they had $0.79 in undistributed taxable income and expect to recognize gains from their equity portfolio in the upcoming quarter.

CSWC also received approval for the second SBIC license, which gives them additional access to capital, up to $175 million to be exact. They also increased their credit facility for the second time this year to $510 million.

This gives me confidence in their ability to not only continue paying dividends but the potential for them to see solid share price growth long-term as they focus on growth.

Risks

As mentioned, BDCs are highly sensitive to the direction of interest rates. Lower base rates are usually a headwind as this results in lower portfolio yields, meaning lower interest income.

To offset this, BDCs can lower fees, see higher portfolio turnover to recognize gains, and invest in riskier companies, increasing spreads. But can result in lower quality. In turn, this could mean higher non-performing loans or the reliance on payment-in-kind income.

All three BDCs are quality companies, but a weakening economy could increase the likelihood of this happening. While I suspect ARCC, BXSL, & CSWC will navigate successfully due to their quality, investors should keep a close eye on this going forward.

Moreover, although all 3 companies have ample liquidity in the form of spillover, this doesn't necessarily equate to a safe dividend. And lower base rates could result in potential dividend cuts in the future.

Another risk is that BDCs are taxed as ordinary income. So, for investors looking into the sector, it may be best to hold in a ROTH rather than a taxable account.

$100K Scenario

The first scenario I use is a $100,000 investment spread evenly across these 3 BDCs at the current prices. The chart below shows how much income an investor could expect to receive on an annual basis.

With the current dividends paid from each, one could expect to receive $10.093.28 annually.

Conclusion

Business Development Companies are often overlooked as great income vehicles.

Although they are not widely known, I suspect BDCs will continue to make for great long-term investments, particularly if traditional bank lending standards continue to tighten in the future.

If you're a retiree looking for additional income or are young and worried about Social Security not being around by the time you reach retirement age, consider investing in BDCs to create an additional income stream.

data.title: PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Value Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

The S&P 500 (SP500, SPX) declined more than -4.2% for the first quarter after falling by over -5.6% in March. It marked the worst quarterly performance for the U.S. stock market since the third quarter of 2022 and was the first down quarter following five consecutive quarters of gains.

During the first quarter, uncertainty flooded the markets as tariffs and trade wars dominated headlines. Investors continued to weigh the inflationary risks posed by tariffs. However, some took a “wait and see” stance, skeptical that tariffs would be permanent, instead viewing tariffs as a negotiation tool aimed at securing more favorable trading conditions.

Bullish investors focused on Trump’s pro-business policies, the strength and resilience of the U.S. economy, earnings growth, increased expectations of rate cuts, and the belief that tariffs are a short-term negotiating tool.

More bearish investors were apprehensive about tariffs and remained concerned over Artificial Intelligence and technology, the selloff in market dominant stocks, trade wars, geopolitical instability, declining consumer confidence, and the growing possibility of a recession.

Within the mid-cap space, value stocks outperformed growth stocks for the quarter (Russell Midcap Value Index: -2.1% versus Russell Midcap Growth Index: -7.1%).

QUARTER

The PGIM Quant Solutions Mid-Cap Value Fund (MUTF:SPRAX) underperformed its benchmark, the Russell Midcap Value Index, in the first quarter of 2025.

U.S. equity markets turned in decidedly negative returns during the first quarter of 2025, as all of the major U.S. indices finished lower, with the exception of large-cap value stocks which managed a small gain. Reversing an earlier trend, growth stocks were the big underperformers, as value stocks proved more resilient across market cap spectrum. Our business prospectors factors underperformed during the quarter, offsetting better performance in our valuation factors. The Fund’s performance is benchmarked in four of the eleven sectors for the quarter.

Contributors to performance included Utilities and Consumer Staples stocks. Utilities were the benchmark’s best performing sector during the quarter, and though the Fund was underweight these stocks, good selection among gas utilities issues drove outperformance in the sector. In Consumer Staples, the Fund’s strong selection among Consumer Staples distributors and food products stocks generated solid performance.

Detracting from the Fund’s performance were Industrial and Information Technology stocks. Among Industrials, the Fund was hurt by lagging returns from machinery and passenger airline stocks. The Fund’s overweight position in the poor performing Information Technology sector, along with difficult selection in the electronic equipment, instruments and components industry, were also a drag on performance.

OUTLOOK & POSITIONING

With the strong performance of U.S. equities in 2024 now in the rear mirror, stocks headed in almost exactly the opposite direction in the first quarter of 2025. Positive returns turned negative, and the growth versus value relationship reversed a long-standing trend of growth outperformance by swinging sharply in favor of cheaper stocks. The only dynamic that remained in-trend was the outperformance of large-cap stocks, and they only did so by the narrowest of margins.

The impressive equity rally in 2024 that was fueled by the AI boom, with multiple companies in the space surpassing the $1 trillion market cap level, reversed sharply during the quarter. Previously the team had called out the risks presented by these stock’s stretched valuations and their heavy concentration in popular capitalization-weighted benchmarks. Those risks were realized, first with the introduction of a seemingly much lower-cost large language AI model named “DEEPSEEK,” and later with the release of sluggish domestic economic data and a new U.S. Presidential administration focused on offsetting trade imbalances with tariffs. While it is true that these were not the only stocks to underperform during a broadly negative quarter, their decline certainly played a significant role in the downturn.

From a broader perspective, while the pullback in the market has discounted some of the risks realized during the quarter, stock valuations remain full, especially among the widely held “Magnificent Seven” stocks in the Information Technology, Communication Services, and Consumer Discretionary sectors. Compounding this, the Federal Reserve’s move to lower interest rates has hit something of a snag as inflationary pressures continue to persist, removing another potential tailwind for equities. Finally, while the potential disruptive events in the “Unknown Unknowns” category we noted in previous writings have emerged in the form of the new U.S. Presidential administration and its policies, we would continue to be cautious about signaling the “all clear” on other potential risk events. Given the risk backdrop, we anticipate more moderate returns and heightened volatility in coming quarters. Within this context, we favor cheaper rather than more expensive stocks, given their deeply discounted valuations, and we believe that the Fund is well positioned to benefit from this dynamic.

data.title: Pharmaceutical Private Equity Deals Face Regulatory Hurdles
data.description:
Global private equity and venture capital investments in pharmaceutical companies lag 2024 figures, and regulatory headwinds are tempering further activity.

Private equity deal value in the pharmaceutical sector reached $7.9 billion across 53 deals from Jan. 1 to May 9, less than half the $20.44 billion from 101 deals in the first five months of 2024, according to S&P Global Market Intelligence data.

Global PE/VC-backed investments in pharmaceuticals, 2021–2025

Quarterly

Annual

Data compiled May 12, 2025.

PE/VC = private equity or venture capital.

* Year to date through May 9, 2025.

Analysis includes global whole-company acquisitions, minority stake acquisitions, and asset acquisitions, and rounds of funding announced between Jan. 1, 2021, and May 9, 2025, where the buyer/investor in the deal is or includes a private equity or venture capital firm, and where the target's primary industry is classified by S&P Global Market Intelligence as pharmaceuticals.

Excludes terminated deals and add-on acquisitions.

Source: S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

US tariffs have become a major obstacle for private equity investments in pharmaceuticals worldwide, according to Arthur D. Little Ltd. principal Rebecka Wadman.

"The US is the largest drug market. If you can't access it, that's tricky," Wadman told Market Intelligence, adding that tariffs make it more difficult for foreign firms to sell to US consumers.

The US government's recent executive order to slash drug prices poses another major challenge to private equity investments in the industry.

"[Prices] drive valuation to a very large extent for a pharmaceutical company," Wadman said. "When you have uncertainty on what that is going to look like, then you need to model different scenarios, and one thing you don't like in a private equity acquisition is to have scenarios that are wildly different from each other."

Limited partners and general partners still find the pharmaceutical industry attractive, with its underlying fundamentals as well as rapid scientific developments that can magnify profits, Wadman said. "But the problem right now is there's a lot of uncertainty in the market."

Asia-Pacific leads in deal value and volume, totaling approximately $3.78 billion across 31 deals so far this year through May 9. Europe follows closely with about $3.36 billion from 11 deals. The US and Canada rank third, with $738 million from 10 deals.

Amid regulatory uncertainty, UK private equity firm Heligan Investments LLP partner Ramesh Jassal believes that the US remains favorable for private equity investment in pharmaceutical companies.

"The US remains a leading destination for pharmaceutical investment, especially in terms of consumption," Jassal told Market Intelligence, citing the robust regulatory framework, strong intellectual property protection and innovation-driven market as advantages over other regions.

However, Jassal expects the weak IPO market to slow private equity exits in the sector, which may restrain deal activity.

"There's IPO hesitancy with companies opting to stay private longer and raise larger private rounds instead of going public, which can limit exit opportunities and slow recycling capital into new deals," Jassal said.

Largest private equity deals

The largest private equity-backed deal in pharmaceuticals so far this year is Bain Capital Pvt. Equity LP's planned $3.36 billion acquisition of Japanese drug manufacturer Mitsubishi Tanabe Pharma Corp. from Mitsubishi Chemical Group Corp.

The second-largest is KKR & Co. Inc.'s (KKR) proposed $2.73 billion purchase of Swedish consumer healthcare company Karo Healthcare AB (OTC:KARBF) from EQT AB (publ).
